mnemonics.title: Data Structure Operations
mnemonics.content: TISD - Traversing, Inserting, Searching, Deleting - THE FOUR BASIC operations to manipulate any data structure.

mnemonics.title: Primitive vs Non-Primitive
mnemonics.content: PINT - Primitive INclude Types (int, float, char, pointer). NON-PRIMITIVE are ADS - Abstract Data Structures (array, linked list, stack, queue, tree, graph).

mnemonics.title: Sparse Matrix Storage
mnemonics.content: ONLY NON-ZERO - Only store what matters! Skip zeros to save memory like skipping empty seats in a theater.

mnemonics.title: Row-Major Address Calculation
mnemonics.content: BASE + i × COLS + j - Think: Row × Full Row Length + Position In Row = Your Seat Number!

mnemonics.title: Structure vs Union
mnemonics.content: SPLIT the room vs UNION as ONE - Structures: SEPARATE rooms for each member. Union: ONE shared room for all!

acronyms.expansion: Coordinate List (COO) - A sparse matrix format storing (row, column, value) triplets
acronyms.usage: Used for sparse matrix representation where each non-zero element stores its coordinates and value

acronyms.expansion: Compressed Sparse Row - Stores non-zero values, column indices, and row pointers
acronyms.usage: Efficient for row-wise operations on sparse matrices

acronyms.expansion: Compressed Sparse Column - Similar to CSR but compressed by columns
acronyms.usage: Efficient for column-wise operations on sparse matrices

acronyms.expansion: Dynamic Memory Allocation - Allocating memory at runtime
acronyms.usage: Using malloc(), calloc(), realloc(), and free() functions in C

visualTips.visualization: Think of an array as a ROW OF LOCKERS in a school corridor. Each locker has a numbered tag (index). You can directly go to locker #5 without opening #1-4 because you know exactly where it is (contiguous memory).
Imagine a STUDENT RECORD FORM with separate boxes for name (string), age (int), and marks (float). Each box has its own space, and they're all grouped under one form (structure).
Think of a UNION as a MULTI-PURPOSE ROOM. It's one big room that can hold either a meeting table OR exercise equipment, but not both at the same time - they share the same space!
Imagine a SPARSE MATRIX like a mostly-empty stadium. Instead of recording every empty seat (zeros), just note down the few occupied seats (non-zeroes) with their row and section numbers.
Row-major is like READING A BOOK page by page (left-to-right, then down). Column-major is like reading a COLUMN at a time (top-to-bottom, then right). Same story, different order!

analogies.analogy: Like a PARKING LOT with numbered spaces. Each car (data element) has a specific spot. You can find any car instantly if you know its spot number - no need to search through every spot.
Like a GPS COORDINATES that points to a specific address. The GPS doesn't contain the house, but it tells you exactly where to find it. Multiple GPS devices can point to the same house.
Like RESERVING A HOTEL ROOM when you arrive, rather than having one pre-assigned before you came. You get exactly what you need at that moment and can check out when done.
Like a EMPLOYEE ID CARD that contains multiple different pieces of information (photo, name, ID number, department) all bound together as one unit.
Like a LARGE INVENTORY LIST where most items show '0 quantity'. Instead of writing every zero, you only list items actually in stock - much shorter and more useful!
Like ADJACENT SEATS in a theater - easy to manage, quick to count, and you can calculate exactly where friend #15 is sitting if you know seat #1 is at position X.
